Implication: Progesterone is a steroid that our body produces and has a range of unique protective effects for our health. Progesterone opposes the HPA axis and estrogen at several levels, inhibits mast cell activity, and is critical for brain growth and development. Progesterone has potent calming, anti-inflammatory, anxiolytic and anti-addiction effects.

Glutathione is known as the body's master antioxidant and detoxifying agent. Its levels tend to decline with inflammatory and oxidative stress driven conditions, and its levels rise when antioxidant status is robust.

The ERMI test (Environmental Relative Moldiness Index) is used to assess mold contamination in indoor environments by analyzing dust samples for DNA of 36 mold species—26 associated with water damage and 10 commonly found indoors. It provides a single numerical score that helps quantify the relative mold burden compared to national baseline levels. This score can guide remediation decisions and is often used in health investigations related to mold exposure.

Folate (vitamin B9) plays a critical role in methylation, which is needed for neurotransmitter production, DNA synthesis, preventing anemia and metabolizing homocysteine. This test measures folate in the cell free portion of the blood (serum).

This panel measures several types of antibodies and RNA levels. Antibodies are proteins that specifically target certain pathogens, while RNA is genetic material from those pathogens. In this instance, both are for tickborne pathogens, including Borrelia (which causes Lyme disease), Bartonella and Cytomegalovirus. Antibodies can be suggestive of a current, recent or past infection, depending on their pattern.

The FIT 132 test measures multiple antibodies and their associated immune responses to a number of different foods. This may provide insight into what foods are triggering inflammation for a certain individual. The test also measures Candida antibodies, as well as markers of intestinal permeability and endotoxin.

Selenium is an essential trace mineral, found in foods like brazil nuts, beef and egg yolks. Selenium serves as a required cofactor in enzymes that activate thyroid hormones and have antioxidant roles. This test measures selenium in the serum, the cell free portion of the blood, and is a good indicator of short term selenium intake. Low serum selenium can lead to a greater risk of hypothyroidism, infection, cognitive issues, arthritis, and several other issues. High levels suggest an excessive dietary intake.

IGF-1 (Insulin-like Growth Factor 1) is a key marker of growth hormone (GH) activity. In young men, low IGF-1 may indicate GH deficiency or poor nutritional/metabolic status, potentially impairing height, muscle growth, and bone density.

Selenium is an essential trace mineral, found in foods like brazil nuts, beef and egg yolks. Selenium serves as a required cofactor in enzymes that activate thyroid hormones and have antioxidant roles. This test measures selenium in the red blood cells, a good indicator of tissue levels or long term selenium storage in the body.

The Advanced Oxidative Stress profile measures multiple indicators of oxidative stress rom whole blood and urine.

Glutathione is the body’s master antioxidant. It is a small peptide that becomes oxidized when defending against oxidative stress. Thus, a higher amount of oxidized glutathione relative to reduced glutathione is indicative of greater oxidative stress.
F2-isoprostane is a product of the peroxidation of polyunsaturated fats. This lipid peroxidation process, and therefore higher F2-isoprostane levels, is a result of oxidative stress.
8-OH-2deoxyguanosine is a product of DNA damage. This process, and therefore higher 8-OH-2deoxyguanosine levels, is a result of oxidative stress.

Diagnostic: ANA Screen IFA with Reflex to Titer and Pattern ($38.86)
Implication: Antinuclear antibodies (ANAs), are immune components that target the cell nucleus. Normally, the immune system generates antibodies to combat foreign proteins, but in conditions of autoimmune disease, the body produces antibodies against the body's own tissue.

Blood pressure is a sensitive metric for stress and healthy metabolism. Blood pressure becomes elevated in response to elevations in the HPA axis, serotonin, adrenaline/noradrenaline, parathyroid hormone and estrogen. It is also directly elevated under conditions of poor glucose metabolism and obesity. Blood pressure tends to be lowered by dietary magnesium, potassium, vitamin D, sunlight exposure, and progesterone.

A metric for the amount of magnesium contained in the cell free portion of the blood. Serum magnesium is not always reflective of magnesium levels in the body, as magnesium is primarily contained within cells with proper metabolism and low stress. Elevated serum magnesium can thus be the result of poor metabolism and stress, causing cells to lose magnesium and having it be retained in the serum.

The primary copper carrying protein in the blood. Copper is vital for mitochondrial energy production and antioxidant function, but in excess it can contribute to oxidative stress. Ceruloplasmin is increased by inflammation and estrogen, while low levels suggest copper deficiency.

Copper is a trace mineral involved in mitochondrial energy production, antioxidant defense, and immune function. However, its levels can become elevated during times of inflammation, as the immune system requires large amounts of copper to kill pathogens and mobilize iron.

Prostate-specific antigen (PSA) is an enzyme secreted by the prostate gland in men. Its levels can rise in cases of prostate cancer or other prostate conditions such as prostatitis or benign prostatic hyperplasia.

Apolipoprotein B (ApoB) is a protein that is attached to molecular "boats" known as lipoproteins. These lipoproteins circulate in the blood and carry many things, most notably cholesterol. The levels of ApoB are thus indicative of the amount of cholesterol carrying lipoproteins in the blood, as opposed to metrics like LDL cholesterol, which measures the amount of cholesterol in these lipoproteins.

Albumin is one of the major circulating proteins in the body, and is normally not present at all in urine. Elevated levels in the urine suggest damage to the kidneys, as they lose their ability to discriminate what gets filtered out in the urine.

Microalbumin is one of the major circulating proteins in the body, and is normally not present at all in urine. Elevated levels in the urine suggest damage to the kidneys, as they lose their ability to discriminate what gets filtered out in the urine.

Cystatin C is a protein that is typically filtered out of the kidney, so elevated levels are suggestive of kidney damage. The Glomerular Filtration Rate (GFR) is a calculation that's often done using the results from the Creatinine test. It provides the best overall assessment of kidney function by estimating how much blood passes through the glomeruli (tiny filters in the kidneys) each minute. Low levels indicate limited filtration capacity, suggesting poor kidney function.

Accumulation of heavy metals can result in a variety of seemingly unexplainable symptoms by inducing oxidative stress and tissue damage, replacing nutritional minerals in proteins and inducing chronic inflammation. This heavy metal panel determines the levels of the key heavy metals in hair. Testing for minerals in hair may provide an idea of their content in cells and tissues, while blood tests only capture their amounts in the transport fluid. Be sure to select "HAIR" and "TOXIC"

The metric which most accurately depicts zinc status. Zinc levels can become depleted with stress, aging, oxidative stress, infection and other conditions which demand more zinc. Zinc is critical for the structure of the gut, can reduce endotoxin and also has anti-inflammatory, antioxidant and pro-androgen effects. Zinc helps to prevent the inactivation of thyroid hormone, promoting the conversion of T4 into the active T3 thyroid hormone. Zinc is also an important nutrient for the immune system, supporting the rapid proliferation, differentiation and antimicrobial properties of the immune cells. Zinc supplementation can also be used to alleviate fungal overgrowths and skin issues like acne, dry skin and eczema.

This panel measures the cellular and extracellular blood levels of nearly every vitamin and mineral, along with a few amino acids, endogenous antioxidants, cofactors, and fatty acids. It is the most comprehensive nutrient evaluation on the market currently. Not only do the levels of these factors imply nutrient deficiencies, they can also provide important information on potential impairments in metabolism.

The Fibrinogen Activity test, also known as the Clauss method or Fibrinogen assay, measures the functional activity of fibrinogen, a protein involved in blood clot formation. Fibrinogen plays a crucial role in maintaining proper coagulation and hemostasis. The Fibrinogen Activity test helps assess the functionality of fibrinogen and is used to evaluate bleeding disorders and monitor certain conditions related to clotting abnormalities.

Potassium is the most abundant mineral inside of cells, typically resident in cells when they are in a properly energized, low stress state. This test measures potassium in the serum, or outside of cells. Thus, elevated levels of serum potassium are indicative of poor energy production or stress, as the cells lose potassium to the extracellular fluids. This can lead to heart beat irregularities and weakness. Low potassium is indicative or poor dietary intake or excessive potassium loss through the kidneys and gut.

25-Hydroxy Vitamin D is the best measurement of vitamin D status. Vitamin D plays vital roles in cell differentiation, the process by which cells become specialized in specific roles. This is especially important for cells of systems in the body that multiply quickly or have multiple different types, like those in the gut, skin and immune system. Vitamin D also controls calcium homeostasis which means it has an impact on cognitive function, heart and bone health. Insufficient vitamin D can lead to a worsening of metabolism, and elevations in several stress hormones.

Vitamin B12 plays a critical role in methylation, which is needed for neurotransmitter production, DNA synthesis, preventing anemia, branch chain amino acid metabolism and clearing homocysteine. Vitamin B12 is synthesized by bacteria, and thus its chronically elevated levels can indicate the presence of bacterial overgrowth in the intestine.

LDL cholesterol is the most common circulating form of cholesterol. LDL cholesterol can be elevated in states of stress or hypothyroidism, since thyroid hormones help utilize cholesterol to produce stress reducing hormones. It can also be too low, which is associated with infection and total mortality.

Triglycerides are the most common type of fat in the blood. Triglycerides are produced by the liver from circulating free fatty acids from fat tissue, which are released during stress and insulin resistance. Triglycerides can be utilized by tissues for energy, or taken back up by fat tissue. High levels can indicate insulin resistance, because insulin suppresses the release of free fatty acids and allows for the re-uptake of them in fat tissue.
The ratio of Triglycerides to HDL cholesterol can be elevated in conditions of impaired glucose metabolism.

The Total Tox Burden Test is a urine-based panel that comprehensively assesses levels of mycotoxins, heavy metals, and environmental toxins in the body. These toxins are capable of inducing oxidative stress, causing mitochondrial dysfunction and disrupting hormonal systems such as the HPA axis and thyroid hormones, leading to countless symptoms. Toxins will likely be tested if there is suspicion of exposure or if symptoms are inexplicable by other causes. High exposure to mycotoxins typically comes from foods like grains, nuts, and coffee, or living in water-damaged buildings with mold. Heavy metals can accumulate from contaminated water, seafood, industrial environments, or old pipes and paint. Environmental toxins are often found in plastics, pesticides, cleaning products, cosmetics, and polluted air.

HDL is type of lipoprotein that carries cholesterol and other fat soluble components to the liver for storage or detoxification. HDL also plays a role in the immune system, dampening the inflammatory response and directly aiding in the clearance of pathogens. It is generally considered to be protective, as low levels can indicate problems with glucose metabolism. However, high levels are a risk as well, potentially indicating a high toxic burden or infection.

This test measures LDL and HDL cholesterol, as well as triglycerides. LDL cholesterol is the most common circulating form of cholesterol. LDL cholesterol can be elevated in states of stress or hypothyroidism, since thyroid hormones help utilize cholesterol to produce stress reducing hormones. It can also be too low, which is associated with infection and total mortality. Triglycerides are the most common type of fat in the blood. High levels can indicate problems with glucose metabolism. HDL is a type of lipoprotein that carries cholesterol and other fat-soluble components to the liver for storage or detoxification. Low levels can indicate problems with glucose metabolism, while high levels can indicate a toxic burden, including endotoxin.
High levels are indicative of hypothyroidism and hyperparathyroidism and impaired steroid synthesis, and thyroid supplementation can lower cholesterol

Absolute Lymphocytes measures the number of lymphocytes. Lymphocytes are white blood cells that exist in both the blood and the lymphatic system and are the keys to adaptive immunity, the recognition and memory of pathogens. Lymphocytes are divided into three types. The B lymphocytes (B cells) are antibody-producing cells that are essential for acquired, antigen-specific immune responses. The second type are T lymphocytes (T cells) some T cells help the body distinguish between "self" and "non-self" antigens while others initiate and control the extent of an immune response, boosting it as needed and then slowing it as the condition resolves. Other types of T cells directly attack and neutralize virus-infected or cancerous cells. The third type are natural killer cells (NK cells) that directly attack and kill abnormal cells such as cancer cells or those infected with a virus. High lymphocyte counts can be the result of infection or stress, while low levels can be the result of vitamin D deficiency.
A type of immune cell that specializes in responding to microbial infection. High levels can indicate an underlying overgrowth of bacteria in the gut or outright infection.

Absolute Basophils measures the number of basophils. Basophils are a type of immune cell that participates in intestinal inflammation and allergies, secreting serotonin and histamine. Levels out of range can indicate an allergenic response or inflammation, as the basophils get produced and used up quickly during these events. High levels can also be the result of stress, while low levels can indicate hypothyroidism.

This test provides multiple metrics that can assess intestinal permeability:

Zonulin: Zonulin is released to initiate intestinal permeability. Elevated levels suggest a "leaky gut" and compromised gut barrier integrity.
DAO (Diamine Oxidase): An enzyme that breaks down histamine in the gut. Low levels can indicate impaired histamine clearance and histamine intolerance.
LPS IgM: Indicates a recent or acute systemic immune response to endotoxin (LPS). Suggests a recent gut barrier breach or infection.
LPS IgG: Reflects past or chronic exposure to endotoxin. Can indicate long-term endotoxin translocation and immune activation.
LPS IgA: Suggests mucosal immune activation in response to LPS.

This test is an at-home analysis of cortisol fluctuation throughout the day. Cortisol is the quintessential stress hormone, which typically fluctuates on a circadian rhythm. It also measures DHEA-S. DHEA is a "youth" steroid with several actions that oppose those of estrogen and the HPA axis. An overproduction of DHEA-S may indicate a compensatory mechanism to stress, while low levels can signify an impairment in its production and increased susceptibility to stress.
The Advanced Adrenal Stress Test provides a good overview of of HPA axis function throughout the day, which is suspected to be dysregulated based on symptoms.

Under healthful conditions, cortisol provides negative feedback to its two precursors, CRH and ACTH. Measuring ACTH alongside cortisol helps provide a full picture of HPA axis activity in a given individual.

DHEA is the most abundant steroid hormone in the body. DHEA levels are high during youth and decline with aging, as it plays a crucial role in opposing the stress systems. DHEA is also a crucial neurosteroid, improving cognitive function and supporting neuron growth and differentiation. An overproduction of DHEA-S may indicate a compensatory mechanism to stress, while low levels can signify an impairment in its production process, potentially due to chronic stress. High-normal levels are associated with superior longevity and disease prevention.

Folate (vitamin B9) plays a critical role in methylation, which is needed for neurotransmitter production, DNA synthesis, preventing anemia and metabolizing homocysteine. This test measures folate in red blood cells.

Estrone sulfate is the most abundant circulating endogenously produced estrogen. While not very active on its own, it provides a more indicative metric for total estrogen levels, acting as a "reservoir" that tissues can take and convert locally into biologically active estrogens.
Prism's Ranges:
WOMEN: (0-6 days) <10-154 ng/dL
(Day 7 to luteal) 15-390 ng/dL
(Luteal) 40-373
(Post menopausal) <10-69 MEN: 12-65

Implication: Estrone is a major circulating estrogen. While it has relatively weak estrogenic activity on its own, it can be converted to stronger estrogens within tissues. The amount of estrone increases with aging and obesity, and is one of the most inflammatory estrogens.
Prism's Ranges:
Female        
  Follicular Phase        10-138 pg/mL
  Luteal Phase        29-113 pg/mL
  Postmenopausal Phase        ≤65 pg/mL

Implication: A measure of the body’s primary estrogen in the blood.

Prolactin is a hormone involved in many stress systems and may be indicative of estrogen or serotonin activity. Prolactin may play a role in intestinal inflammation, poor Vitamin D status, hypothyroidism, sexual dysfunction and general anhedonia as it is inversely related to the reward systems.
This panel provides several metrics that can illustrate a basic overview of an individual’s kidney health.

Luteinizing hormone (LH) is a hormone secreted by the pituitary as a part of the systemic signaling system that produces our gonadal hormones: estrogens and progesterone in women and androgens in men. A high LH is indicative of hypogonadism, since the system exists in negative feedback, i.e high gonadal hormones should result in suppression of the precursor pituitary hormones.

Follicle Stimulating Hormone (FSH) is a hormone secreted by the pituitary as a part of the systemic signaling system that produces our gonadal hormones: estrogens and progesterone in women and androgens in men. A high FSH is indicative of hypogonadism, since the system exists in negative feedback, i.e high gonadal hormones should result in low precursor pituitary hormones.

Follicle Stimulating Hormone (FSH) & Luteinizing hormone (LH) are hormones secreted by the pituitary as a part of the systemic signaling system that produces our gonadal hormones: estrogens and progesterone in women and androgens in men. High levels of these hormones are indicative of hypogonadism, since the system exists in negative feedback, i.e high gonadal hormones should result in suppression of the precursor pituitary hormones.

Testosterone is the primary androgenic hormone, mediating functions like libido, muscular development and strength, fat loss, cognitive and digestive function. Testosterone production is positively influenced by sufficient calorie intake, dietary carbohydrate (due to insulin), vitamin D, zinc, vitamin A, resistance training and proper sleep, and can be impaired due to oxidative stress, hypothyroidism and inflammation. Elevated testosterone in women is potentially a sign of polycystic ovarian syndrome, a condition of high androgens typically caused by insulin resistance and stress.

Testosterone is the primary androgenic hormone, mediating functions like libido, muscular development and strength, fat loss, cognitive and digestive function. Testosterone production is positively influenced by sufficient calorie intake, dietary carbohydrate (due to insulin), vitamin D, zinc, vitamin A, resistance training and proper sleep, and can be impaired due to oxidative stress, hypothyroidism and inflammation.

The total and biologically available amount of testosterone, indicative of overall androgenic activity. Poor glucose metabolism, low vitamin D or zinc status, a lack of saturated fat or carbohydrate (especially from starch), chronic inflammation and stress can all impair testosterone output.

Diagnostic: Testosterone, Total and Free; and Sex Hormone Binding Globulin ($58.95)
Implication: The total and unbound amounts of testosterone, indicative of overall androgenic activity, as well as SHBG, the primary protein which sequesters androgens and estrogens, leaving them unavailable to exert their actions. Elevated levels of SHBG can indicate a lack of insulin signaling from carbohydrates, while low levels can indicate chronically elevated insulin levels.

Free testosterone measures the unbound amounts of the primary androgen. Testosterone, can be bound to sex hormone binding globulin, sequestering it from exerting biological actions. This can lead to low free testosterone.

A measure of the amount of lactic acid in the blood at rest. Lactic acid can be chronically elevated with certain impairments in energy metabolism, and thus can give information on where such an impairment is, along with other symptoms and tests. Some classical causes of high lactic acid are poor oxygen delivery to tissues (anemia) or infection, as both of these factors can impair energy production. Liver dysfunction can also create high lactic acid, since the liver clears it out.

Gliadin is a fragment of the gluten protein. IgG and IgA are two of the most abundant classes of antibodies in the body, which are immune proteins that have specific targets they are created for and bind to as a part of the immune response. The presence of gliadin IgG or IgA antibodies can indicate exposure to, and potentially an inflammatory response towards, gluten.

Creatinine is a waste product of creatine, an important component of energy production in the brain and muscles, that must be excreted by the kidneys. Thus, high levels of creatinine are indicative of poor kidney function. The Glomerular Filtration Rate (GFR) is a calculation that's often done using the results from the Creatinine test. It provides the best overall assessment of kidney function by estimating how much blood passes through the glomeruli (tiny filters in the kidneys) each minute.

Sex hormone binding globulin (SHBG) is the primary protein which sequesters androgens and estrogens, leaving them unavailable to exert their actions. Elevated levels of SHBG can be the result of excess estrogen, lack of carbohydrate intake, liver issues, and is generally seen in aging. Low levels of SHBG can be indicative of hypothyroidism and stress, and is a risk for several diseases including cardiovascular disease and even cancer.

Dihydrotestosterone (DHT) is the strongest androgen in the body. It is made from testosterone. DHT has potent anti-stress, cognitive enhancing and relaxing effects, while protecting against gut inflammation.

Calcium is an essential mineral vital for a variety of functions in the body: transmitting key signals within tissues and building bones and teeth. Calcium levels in the blood are primarily regulated by parathyroid hormone, which acts to liberate calcium from hard tissues into the blood. Parathyroid hormone is lowered when there is sufficient vitamin D, dietary calcium, and magnesium, and thus elevated calcium levels can suggest insufficiencies of these nutrients.

Calcium is an essential mineral vital for a variety of functions in the body: transmitting key signals within tissues and building bones and teeth. Calcium levels in the blood are primarily regulated by parathyroid hormone (PTH), which acts to liberate calcium from hard tissues into the blood. Parathyroid hormone is lowered when there is sufficient vitamin D, dietary calcium, and magnesium, and thus elevated calcium levels can suggest insufficiencies of these nutrients. PTH can slow the rate of metabolism and is indicative of metabolic stress. Hyperparathyroidism has many digestive manifestations including constipation, heartburn, nausea and loss of appetite.

Copper and zinc are two essential minerals necessary for a myriad of functions throughout the body. Copper and zinc tend to have an antagonistic relationship with each other, as they impede each others' absorption and transport throughout the body. Excess copper relative to zinc is typically a sign of inflammation and can lead to tissue damage. An elevated copper to zinc ratio is seen in aging, cancer, developmental disorders, and is inversely related to longevity. Low dietary zinc can also contribute to an elevated copper to zinc ratio.

Diagnostic: Free T3, Free T4, RT3 and TSH Panel ($68.74)
Implication:
Free T4 is a measure of the protein-unbound precursor thyroid hormone T4. T4 is the primary hormone released from the thyroid gland. T4 is largely inactive metabolically and needs to be converted in other tissues to the active thyroid hormone, T3. T4 is often carried in the bloodstream via binding proteins, however some of the T4 is not bound to any protein, making it more readily taken up by tissues. The Free T4 metric is a measurement of the unbound T4. High levels can indicate excessive supplementation, hyperthyroidism, or a poor conversion of T4 into T3. Low levels are suggestive of primary hypothyroidism.

Free T3 is a measure of the protein-unbound active thyroid hormone T3. T3 is a thyroid hormone released in small amounts from the thyroid gland but is primarily produced by other tissues from T4. T3 is the thyroid hormone primarily responsible for raising the metabolic rate. Like T4, T3 is often carried in the bloodstream via binding proteins, however some of the T3 is not bound to any protein, making it more readily taken up by tissues. The Free T3 metric is a measurement of the unbound T3. High levels can indicate excessive supplementation, or hyperthyroidism. Low levels are suggestive of functional hypothyroidism.

Thyroid stimulating hormone (TSH) is made by the pituitary gland as apart of thyroid hormone production. It causes the thyroid gland to secrete thyroid hormones T4 and T3, and these in turn lower the output of TSH. Thus, high levels of TSH are indicative of poor thyroid hormone output. Even slightly elevated TSH has been shown to be a risk for impaired metabolic function and stress. TSH can also be lowered by stress hormones, so it should be interpreted alongside other thyroid metrics and symptoms.

Reverse T3 (RT3) is a thyroid hormone that is produced in response to stress, inflammation, poor liver function and nutrition from T4. It facilitates the opposite functions of active thyroid hormone, suppressing metabolism.

Thyroid stimulating hormone (TSH) is made by the pituitary gland as a part of thyroid hormone production. It causes the thyroid gland to secrete thyroid hormones T4 and T3, and these in turn lower the output of TSH. Thus, high levels of TSH are indicative of poor thyroid hormone output. Even slightly elevated TSH has been shown to be a risk for impaired metabolic function and stress. TSH can also be lowered by stress hormones, so it should be interpreted alongside other thyroid metrics and symptoms.

This panel provides several metrics that can illustrate a basic overview of an individual’s liver health. Included metrics: 
Gamma glutamyl transferase (GGT) - an enzyme found in the highest amounts in the liver. Injury to the liver results in release of the substance into the blood. 
Albumin - a protein made by the liver, low levels indicate poor liver function. 
Albumin/Globulin Ratio 
Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) is an enzyme that plays a critical role in maintaining intestinal homeostasis, reducing inflammation, and detoxifying endotoxin. Elevated levels suggest liver damage or intestinal inflammation.
Alanine transaminase (ALT) - an enzyme found in the highest amounts in the liver. Injury to the liver results in release of the substance into the blood. 
AST (aspartate aminotransferase) is an enzyme found in high amounts in liver, heart, and muscle cells. It is also found in lesser amounts in other tissues. Injury to the liver results in release of the substance into the blood.
Bilirubin - a yellowish pigment found in bile, a fluid made by the liver. A small amount of older red blood cells are replaced by new blood cells every day. Bilirubin is left after these older blood cells are removed. The liver helps break down bilirubin so that it can be removed from the body in the stool. Thus, high levels of bilirubin indicate poor liver function. 
Globulin - blood proteins other than albumin. Identifying the types of globulins can help diagnose certain disorders. Globulins are roughly divided into three groups: alpha, beta, and gamma globulins. Gamma globulins include various types of antibodies such as immunoglobulins (Ig) M, G, and A.

Secretory IgA is secreted by the gut as a part of the gastrointestinal immune system, representing the first line of defense of the gut against bacteria. Elevated levels of sIgA have been associated with an upregulated immune response, while low levels can be the result of stress and impaired immunity.

Immunoglobulin A is an antibody that plays a vital role in promoting the immune response to infectious agents like bacteria and viruses. IgA levels can become depleted due to HPA axis overactivity, other chronic stressors, infection and vitamin A or D deficiency.

Immunoglobulin G is an antibody that plays a vital role in promoting the immune response to infectious agents like bacteria and viruses. IgG levels can become depleted due to HPA axis overactivity, other chronic stressors, infection and vitamin A or D deficiency.

Immunoglobulin M is an antibody that plays a vital role in promoting the immune response to infectious agents like bacteria and viruses. IgM levels can become depleted due to HPA axis overactivity, other chronic stressors, infection and vitamin A or D deficiency.

The Lyme immunoglobulin M (IgM) antibodies are proteins that specifically target the Borrelia burgdorferi bacteria, which causes Lyme disease. IgM antibody reactivity suggests a recent infection by this bacteria.

The Lyme immunoglobulin G (IgG) antibodies are proteins that specifically target the Borrelia burgdorferi bacteria, which causes Lyme disease. IgG antibody reactivity suggests a past infection by this bacteria. In the absence of IgM antibodies, it is suggestive of an infection several months prior to testing.

Immunoglobulin subclass IgG4 is typically linked to chronic antigen exposure, such as during immunotherapy, or in cases of parasitic infection. It has also been connected to several autoimmune conditions, as well as asthma and specific allergies. Elevated levels can indicate a prolonged or repeated immune response to these factors.

Vitamin B1 is a nutrient used primarily in order to generate energy and support the antioxidant system. Low levels of B1 have been implicated in dysfunction of the nervous system, such as pain, weakness or tingling in peripheral nerves, cognitive impairment, and slowed gut motility.
T3 uptake is a metric for how much active thyroid hormone is biologically available to the body. Low amounts can be driven by excess estrogen.

Reverse T3 is a thyroid hormone that is produced in response to stress, low carbohydrate intake, inflammation, poor liver function and nutrition from T4. It facilitates the opposite functions of active thyroid hormone, suppressing metabolism.
Occult blood is microscopic blood found in the stool. A positive result is suggestive of intestinal inflammation or bleeding.
Calprotectin is an antimicrobial protein released by immune cells. Elevated levels in the stool suggest intestinal inflammation, pathogenic overgrowth or infection.
CH4 (methane) is a gas produced by bacteria in the gut. Excess methane producing bacteria can slow motility and contribute to symptoms like bloating, gas, constipation and discomfort.

T4 is the primary hormone released from the thyroid gland. T4 is largely inactive metabolically, and needs to be converted in other tissues to the active thyroid hormone, T3. T4 is often carried in the bloodstream via binding proteins, however some of the T4 is not bound to any protein, making it more readily taken up by tissues. The Free T4 metric is a measurement of the unbound T4. High levels can indicate excessive supplementation, hyperthyroidism, or a poor conversion of T4 into T3. Low levels are suggestive of primary hypothyroidism.

T3 is a thyroid hormone released in small amounts from the thyroid gland, but is primarily produced by other tissues from T4. T3 is the thyroid hormone primarily responsible for raising the metabolic rate. T3 is often carried in the bloodstream via binding proteins, however some of the T3 is not bound to any protein, making it more readily taken up by tissues. The Free T3 metric is a measurement of the unbound T3. High levels can indicate excessive supplementation, or hyperthyroidism. Low levels are suggestive of functional hypothyroidism.

Thyroid stimulating immunoglobulins (TSI) are autoantibodies, components of the immune system that specifically target the thyroid gland and mimic the functions of thyroid stimulating hormone (TSH). TSH is made by the pituitary gland as apart of thyroid hormone production. It causes the thyroid gland to secrete thyroid hormones T4 and T3, and these in turn lower the output of TSH. Thus, high levels of TSH are inversely related to thyroid gland stimulation. Antibodies towards the thyroid indicate that the body is mounting an immune response to its own tissue, suggestive of an autoimmune thyroid condition. Autoimmune conditions have been consistently linked to estrogen, stress, and inflammation stemming from the gut. Autoimmunity can also arise from a lack of vitamin A, C, and D, as well as zinc, as these nutrients act synergistically to shift the balance of immune cells towards regulation and away from inflammation.

Thyroid peroxidase and thyroglobulin are proteins involved in the production of thyroid hormones in the thyroid gland. Antibodies against this proteins indicate that the body is mounting an immune response to its own tissue, suggestive of an autoimmune thyroid condition. Autoimmune conditions have been consistently linked to estrogen, stress, and inflammation stemming from the gut. Autoimmunity can also arise from a lack of vitamin A, C, and D, as well as zinc, as these nutrients act synergistically to shift the balance of immune cells towards regulation and away from inflammation.

Thyroid peroxidase is an enzyme involved in the production of thyroid hormones in the thyroid gland. Antibodies against this enzyme indicate that the body is mounting an immune response to its own tissue, suggestive of an autoimmune thyroid condition. Autoimmune conditions have been consistently linked to estrogen, stress, and inflammation stemming from the gut. Autoimmunity can also arise from a lack of vitamin A, C, and D, as well as zinc, as these nutrients act synergistically to shift the balance of immune cells towards regulation and away from inflammation.

Thyroglobulin is a protein involved in the production of thyroid hormones in the thyroid gland. Antibodies against this enzyme indicate that the body is mounting an immune response to its own tissue, suggestive of an autoimmune thyroid condition. Autoimmune conditions have been consistently linked to estrogen, stress, and inflammation stemming from the gut. Autoimmunity can also arise from a lack of vitamin A, C, and D, as well as zinc, as these nutrients act synergistically to shift the balance of immune cells towards regulation and away from inflammation.
Measures levels of daily adrenaline and noradrenaline activity.

Cortisol is the quintessential stress hormone, released from the adrenals in the final step of the HPA axis. Elevated morning cortisol and cortisol to DHEA-S ratio are excellent predictors of stress, metabolic and immune function, and mortality. A low morning cortisol is indicative of adrenal insufficiency, where the adrenal glands do not produce enough cortisol as an adaptive response to chronic stress.

Creatine Kinase is an enzyme found primarily in the heart and muscles. High levels in the blood are indicative of damage to these tissues as they “leak” the enzyme into systemic circulation. This damage can be driven by excessive exercise, endotoxin, or oxidative stress.

This panel provides several metrics that can illustrate a basic overview of an individual’s metabolic health. It includes the following measures:

Albumin is a protein made by the liver, one of the major type of proteins in the blood. Low levels indicate poor liver function.
Globulin is the other major type of protein in the blood. High levels may reflect overproduction of globulins, indicative of an immune response to infection or stress.
Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) an enzyme that plays a critical role in maintaining intestinal homeostasis, reducing inflammation, and detoxifying endotoxin. Elevated levels suggest liver damage or intestinal inflammation.
Alanine transaminase (ALT) is an enzyme found in the highest amounts in the liver. Injury to the liver results in release of the substance into the blood. 
Aspartate aminotransferase (AST) is an enzyme found in high amounts in liver, heart, and muscle cells. It is also found in lesser amounts in other tissues. 
Bilirubin is a yellowish pigment found in bile, a fluid made by the liver. A small amount of older red blood cells are replaced by new blood cells every day. Bilirubin is left after these older blood cells are removed. The liver helps break down bilirubin so that it can be removed from the body in the stool. 
Calcium is an essential mineral vital for a variety of functions in the body: transmitting key signals within tissues and building bones and teeth. Calcium levels in the blood are primarily regulated by parathyroid hormone, which acts to liberate calcium from hard tissues into the blood. Parathyroid hormone is lowered when there is sufficient vitamin D, dietary calcium, and magnesium, and thus elevated calcium levels can suggest insufficiencies of these nutrients. 
Carbon dioxide is both a result and driver of efficient energy production. Low levels may indicate impaired glucose metabolism, or from excess systemic acidity, since carbon dioxide helps to form our endogenous acid buffering agent. Carbon dioxide production is also the rate limiting step in the production of stomach acid.
Creatinine is a waste product of creatine, an important component of energy production in the brain and muscles, that must be excreted by the kidneys. Thus, high levels of creatinine are indicative of poor kidney function. 
Glomerular Filtration Rate (GFR) is a calculation that's often done using the results from the Creatinine test. It provides the best overall assessment of kidney function by estimating how much blood passes through the glomeruli (tiny filters in the kidneys) each minute. 
Glucose is the body’s primary source of fuel. Low levels may suggest issues with the liver, since this organ is responsible for storing and producing glucose in between meals, whereas high levels are indicative of general inadequate metabolism. 
Potassium is the most abundant mineral inside of cells, typically resident in cells when they are in a properly energized, low stress state. This test measures potassium in the serum, or outside of cells. Thus, elevated levels of serum potassium are indicative of poor energy production or stress, as the cells lose potassium to the extracellular fluids. This can lead to heart beat irregularities and weakness. Low potassium is indicative or poor dietary intake or excessive potassium loss through the kidneys and gut. 
Total protein is the total amount of two classes of proteins, albumin and globulin that are found in the fluid portion of your blood. Proteins are important parts of all cells and tissues. Your albumin helps prevent fluid from leaking out of blood vessels and your globulins are an important part of your immune system. 
Sodium is the most abundant mineral outside of cells when cells are in a properly energized, low stress state. This test measures sodium outside of cells. Elevated levels of sodium are indicative of poor energy production or stress, as the stress systems can cause sodium retention. Low sodium is indicative or poor dietary intake or excessive potassium loss through the kidneys and gut. 
Blood urea nitrogen (BUN) is what forms when protein breaks down. Stress hormones, such as cortisol, adrenaline and noradrenaline break down our tissue protein, which gets metabolized by the liver and excretes urea-nitrogen as a byproduct. Thus, a high BUN can indicate elevations in these mediators. A high BUN can also indicate impaired kidney function as they are responsible for excreting the excess nitrogen from the blood.
This measures damage to red blood cells from blood sugar, high levels indicate poor glucose metabolism.
Insulin is the primary hormone involved in the metabolism of glucose. Elevated levels while fasting are indicative of impaired glucose metabolism.

This panel evaluates a person's exposure to various types of hepatitis viruses by measuring their corresponding antibodies. The test helps identify current or past infections, and assess the extent of liver damage.

Ammonia is a toxic byproduct of protein metabolism. Typically, there is very little ammonia circulating because the liver detoxifies ammonia efficiently. However, this process can become impaired due to poor mitochondrial function, manganese or biotin deficiency or genetic predisposition. Elevated ammonia can result from any of these causes, as well as excessive protein intake or liver issues. Ammonia is also produced by some pathogenic bacteria in the gut, which may be a contributor to high ammonia as well.

Carbon dioxide is both a result and driver of efficient energy production. Low levels may indicate impaired glucose metabolism, or from excess systemic acidity, since carbon dioxide helps to form our endogenous acid buffering agent. This acidity can arise from renal (kidney) loss of carbon dioxide or from excess metabolic acid production. Carbon dioxide production is also the rate limiting step in the production of stomach acid.

A metric for the amount of magnesium contained within cells. Low levels may indicate poor metabolism or stress, since intracellular magnesium can be lost in these conditions. Low levels may also indicate a dietary insufficiency of magnesium. Retaining intracellular magnesium is critical for nearly every function in the body, including hormonal health, maintaining healthy bowel movements, immunity, and relaxation.

LDH is an enzyme found in almost all body tissues, including the heart, liver, muscles, kidneys, and red blood cells. The LDH test provides information about tissue damage or disease processes occurring in various organs.

Diagnostic: Non-invasive Breath Testing to Evaluate Bacterial Overgrowth ($200)
Implication: This test measures the amount of certain gases in the breath, which are products of bacterial fermentation in the small intestine. Small intestinal bacterial overgrowth can alter motility and lead to various digestive symptoms.

The Gut Zoomer by Vibrant Wellness is a comprehensive stool test that assesses microbiome balance, pathogens, inflammation, and gut barrier integrity. lts methodology allows for detection and quantification of over 300 microbes, including bacteria, fungi, viruses, and parasites.
Suspected bacterial, fungal or parasitic overgrowths, which contribute to numerous systemic and gastrointestinal symptoms, can be detected using stool samples.
This is an enzyme that is produced in the gut and degrades histamine, thereby reducing its associated issues.

Mast cells, when activated, can produce histamine, which plays a role in various gastrointestinal symptoms. These histamine producing cells tend to be active in greater quantities in FGIDs. Histamine can also be produced by bacterial fermentation in the gut.

Diagnostic: Complete Blood Count (CBC) with Differential and Platelets Blood Test ($5.95)
Implication:
This panel measures the amounts and parameters of main components of the blood: red blood cells, white blood cells, and platelets. The CBC can elucidate various underlying issues, primarily pertaining to iron status, anemia, immune function and inflammation. It includes the following measures:

Hematocrit is a blood test that determines the proportion of red blood cells in the total blood volume. This measurement is influenced by both the quantity and the size of red blood cells. A low hematocrit is indicative of anemia, where there is a lack of red blood cells, which deliver oxygen around the body to produce energy. This can lead to symptoms such as fatigue and shortness of breath. Anemia can be driven by numerous factors, including a lack of iron, or vitamins B2, B9 or B12, hypothyroidism, chronic inflammation, and stress. High hematocrit can be the result of low oxygen delivery into cells, resulting in a compensatory overproduction of red blood cells.
Hemoglobin is the primary oxygen carrying protein in our red blood cells, fueling the body's metabolism. Hemoglobin level is highly dependent on iron status, so low levels can suggest iron deficiency anemia due to poor dietary intake, inflammation or stress, but elevated levels of hemoglobin may be an adaptive response to low oxygen availability.
Mean corpuscular hemoglobin (MCH) is an average measure of the hemoglobin content per red blood cell. It adds additional context to the hemoglobin measure, elucidating the amount of hemoglobin relative to the amount of red blood cells. Low levels can suggest iron deficiency anemia due to poor dietary intake, inflammation or stress, but elevated levels of hemoglobin may be an adaptive response to low oxygen availability. High levels can also be indicative of megaloblastic anemia, where red blood cells become enlarged specifically due to deficiencies of vitamins B9 and B12, and thus each cell carries more hemoglobin than normal.
Mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration (MCHC) indicates the concentration of hemoglobin per unit volume within a red blood cell. Unlike MCH, MCHC is the hemoglobin content relative to the cell's volume rather than the number of cells. MCHC does not become elevated in megaloblastic anemia, as the hemoglobin to cell volume proportion remains constant even if the cell volume increases. Low levels still point to iron deficiency anemia due to poor dietary intake, inflammation or stress.
Mean corpuscular volume (MCV) is a measurement of the average size (volume) of red blood cells. Low MCV can indicate iron deficiency anemia due to poor dietary intake, inflammation or stress, or heavy metal poisoning. High MCV can be a result of hypothyroidism or megaloblastic anemia, driven by vitamin B9 or B12 deficiency. 
Red cell distribution width (RDW) is a calculation of variation in size of red blood cells. When it is high, it can point to being in the early stages of either iron deficiency or megaloblastic anemia, depending on MCV, MCHC and MCH, as some cells are affected in size while others have not been.
A Red Blood Cell (RBC) count is a blood test that tells how many red blood cells you have. RBCs contain hemoglobin, which carries oxygen to tissues to fuel energy metabolism. Low RBC counts indicate anemia due to any cause, while high levels can indicate poor tissue oxygenation. High RBC can also indicate parasitic infection, as these pathogens can invade red blood cells and cause the body to produce more adaptively.
A white blood cell (WBC) count measures the total number of white blood cells (WBCs), which are immune cells. A high WBC can be indicative of a bacterial, viral or fungal infection or other underlying inflammation. Low WBC can be the result of chronic infection as the immune cells become depleted fighting pathogens, or as a result of vitamin D deficiency or hypothyroidism. 
Platelets are cells that facilitate blood clotting as a part of the inflammatory or wound healing process. Measures above normal can suggest the presence of chronic inflammation.
Mean Platelet Volume (MPV) measures the average size of the platelets. A low MPV can be suggestive of an inflammatory response as the body is producing newer, small platelets.
Absolute neutrophils measures the number of all types of neutrophils. Neutrophils measures the percentage of white blood cells that are neutrophils. Neutrophils are one of the major white blood cells, involved in the response to infectious agents, such as bacteria, fungus and viruses. Their levels being out of range can be indicative of a systemic bacterial or viral infection. A high neutrophil count can be indicative of a bacterial, viral or fungal infection or other underlying inflammation. Low neutrophils can be the result of chronic infection as the immune cells become depleted fighting pathogens, or as a result of B9, B12 or copper deficiency.
Absolute Basophils measures the number of basophils. Basophils measures the percentage of white blood cells that are basophils. Basophils are a type of immune cell that participates in intestinal inflammation and allergies, secreting serotonin and histamine. High levels can indicate an allergenic response or inflammation.
Absolute Eosinophils measures the number of eosinophils. Eosinophils measures the percentage of white blood cells that are eosinophils. Eosinophils are immune cells that respond to infections caused by parasites and play a role in allergic reactions. Elevated levels can indicate parasitic infection or allergy.
Absolute Lymphocytes measures the number of lymphocytes. Lymphocytes measures the percentage of white blood cells that are lymphocytes. Lymphocytes are white blood cells that exist in both the blood and the lymphatic system and are the keys to adaptive immunity, the recognition and memory of pathogens. Lymphocytes are divided into three types. The B lymphocytes (B cells) are antibody-producing cells that are essential for acquired, antigen-specific immune responses. The second type are T lymphocytes (T cells) some T cells help the body distinguish between "self" and "non-self" antigens while others initiate and control the extent of an immune response, boosting it as needed and then slowing it as the condition resolves. Other types of T cells directly attack and neutralize virus-infected or cancerous cells. The third type are natural killer cells (NK cells) that directly attack and kill abnormal cells such as cancer cells or those infected with a virus. High lymphocyte counts can be the result of infection or stress, while low levels can be the result of vitamin D deficiency.
Mean Platelet Volume (MPV) measures the average size of the platelets. A low MPV can be suggestive of an inflammatory response as the body is producing newer, small platelets.

Platelets are cells that facilitate blood clotting as a part of the inflammatory or wound healing process. Measures above normal can suggest the presence of chronic inflammation. Low levels can suggest deficiencies of nutrients like iron and vitamins B9 and B12, or due to infection.

Hematocrit is a blood test that determines the proportion of red blood cells in the total blood volume. This measurement is influenced by both the quantity and the size of red blood cells. A low hematocrit is indicative of anemia, where there is a lack of red blood cells, which deliver oxygen around the body to produce energy. This can lead to symptoms such as fatigue and shortness of breath. Anemia can be driven by numerous factors, including a lack of iron, or vitamins B2, B9 or B12, hypothyroidism, chronic inflammation, and stress. High hematocrit can be the result of low oxygen delivery into cells, resulting in a compensatory overproduction of red blood cells.

Zonulin is a protein that helps seal the lining of the intestine together. Zonulin prevents permeability of the gut lining to bacteria and food antigens, which can cause intestinal and systemic inflammation. High zonulin levels are indicative of a breakdown of this barrier.
Bilirubin is a component of bile acids, which are produced by the liver. High bilirubin suggests damage to the liver, as it leaks out from the liver into the blood.
Absolute Blasts measures the number of blasts. Blasts are immature forms of white blood cells. High levels can indicate the presence of an underlying infection.
Blasts measures the pecentage of white blood cells that are blasts. Blasts are immature forms of white blood cells. High levels can indicate the presence of an underlying infection.

Absolute neutrophils measures the number of all types of neutrophils. Neutrophils are one of the major white blood cells, involved in the response to infectious agents, such as bacteria, fungus and viruses. A high neutrophil count can be indicative of a bacterial, viral or fungal infection or other underlying inflammation. Low neutrophils can be the result of chronic infection as the immune cells become depleted fighting pathogens, or as a result of B9, B12 or copper deficiency.

Absolute band neutrophils measures the number of band neutrophils. Neutrophils are one of the major white blood cells, involved in the response to infectious agents, such as bacteria, fungus and viruses. Band neutrophils are immature, young neutrophils. Their levels being out of range can be indicative of a systemic bacterial or viral infection.

Band neutrophils measures the percentage of white blood cells that are band neutrophils. Neutrophils are one of the major white blood cells, involved in the response to infectious agents, such as bacteria, fungus and viruses. Band neutrophils are immature, young neutrophils. Their levels being out of range can be indicative of a systemic bacterial or viral infection.

Reactive Lymphocytes measures lymphocytes that have been "activated" due to antigen stimulation. Lymphocytes are divided into three types. The B lymphocytes (B cells) are antibody-producing cells that are essential for acquired, antigen-specific immune responses. The second type are T lymphocytes (T cells) some T cells help the body distinguish between "self" and "non-self" antigens while others initiate and control the extent of an immune response, boosting it as needed and then slowing it as the condition resolves. Other types of T cells directly attack and neutralize virus-infected or cancerous cells. The third type are natural killer cells (NK cells) that directly attack and kill abnormal cells such as cancer cells or those infected with a virus.

A white blood cell (WBC) count measures the total number of white blood cells (WBCs), which are immune cells. A high WBC count can be indicative of a bacterial, viral or fungal infection or other underlying inflammation. Low WBC count can be the result of chronic infection as the immune cells become depleted fighting pathogens, or as a result of B9, B12 or copper deficiency.
Aldolase is an enzyme concentrated within the liver and muscles primarily, and thus its elevation in the blood suggests damage to these tissues.
AST is an enzyme concentrated within the liver primarily, and thus its elevation in the blood suggests damage to the liver. This damage is a result of oxidative stress and inflammation.
ALT is an enzyme concentrated within the liver primarily, and thus its elevation in the blood suggests damage to the liver. This damage is a result of oxidative stress and inflammation.
GGT is an enzyme concentrated within the liver primarily, and thus its elevation in the blood suggests damage to the liver.

Implication: Directly measuring stomach acid is not widely available, however this test can help confirm a suspicion of low stomach acid from various symptoms.

Diagnostic: Stomach acid at home test: Take one pill of Betaine HCl (can be purchased here) with a meal with protein per day. If acid reflux or burning pain is experienced, then stomach acid production is adequate. If not, increase to 2 pills before a meal with protein. If there is still no burning or acid reflux, then continue to increase by one pill per day before a meal. If 3-4 pills must be taken before experiencing any reflux, then low stomach acid production is likely.

A Red Blood Cell (RBC) count is a blood test that tells how many red blood cells you have. RBCs contain hemoglobin, which carries oxygen to tissues to fuel energy metabolism. Low RBC counts indicate anemia due to any cause, while high levels can indicate poor tissue oxygenation. High RBC can also indicate parasitic infection, as these pathogens can invade red blood cells and cause the body to produce more adaptively.

Glucose is the body’s primary source of fuel. Low levels may suggest issues with the liver, since this organ is responsible for storing and producing glucose in between meals. Low levels can also be due to a biotin or manganese deficiency, since these nutrients are required for the liver to make glucose during periods without any dietary carbohydrate. High levels are indicative of inadequate glucose metabolism, stress or insulin resistance.

Hemoglobin is the primary oxygen carrying protein in our red blood cells, fueling the body's metabolism. Hemoglobin level is highly dependent on iron status, but elevated levels of hemoglobin may be an adaptive response to low oxygen availability.

This is a metric of how much iron the proteins in your blood are capable of binding, and thus it is inversely related to the total amount of iron stores in the body. A high UIBC is indicative of iron deficiency, while a low TIBC is indicative of iron excess.

This is a metric of how much iron the proteins in your blood are capable of binding, and thus it is inversely related to the total amount of iron stores in the body. A high TIBC is indicative of iron deficiency, while a low TIBC is indicative of iron excess.

A pattern of low MCV, high RBC count, normal iron, and normal or high hemoglobin is classic for thalassemia trait. Thalassemia is an inherited defect in globin chain production that leads to small red cells with less hemoglobin per cell, but the bone marrow compensates by making many more RBCs, keeping total hemoglobin normal or high. Hemoglobin electrophoresis is useful here because it can detect abnormal hemoglobin fractions. For example, β-thalassemia trait typically shows elevated HbA₂, confirming the diagnosis and distinguishing it from other microcytic conditions. Most people with trait are asymptomatic, though some may experience mild fatigue, pallor, or slightly reduced exercise tolerance, especially during illness or pregnancy.

Transferrin is the major iron carrying proteins in the body. High transferrin can indicate iron deficiency, if it is accompanied by a relatively high iron binding capacity and low saturation. Low transferrin is indicative of iron excess.
Refers to the proportion of the transferrin protein that contains iron. Low levels suggest iron deficiency, while high levels can suggest iron overload.

Ferritin is the primary protein that stores iron in cells. Its levels being elevated in the serum (which does not contain cells) indicates cell damage and inflammation, as the protein "leaks" into the serum and also participates in the inflammatory response. High levels can also indicate iron overload, while low levels can be the result of iron deficiency.

Pregnenolone is one of the “youth” steroids that declines with aging and stress. It is perhaps the body’s primary endogenous anti-stress hormone. It can lower HPA axis function, counteract excess estrogen, enhances mitochondrial function and structure, and has some potent anti-inflammatory properties. Pregnenolone also has several neuroprotective, cognitive, anti-addiction and mood enhancing properties. Metabolites of pregnenolone have a key gut protective effect and become depleted when the gut becomes inflamed. Pregnenolone is the “mother of all steroid hormones,” from which all other steroid hormones are synthesized.

Iron deficiency can create symptoms such as fatigue, while excess iron can drive tissue damage and inflammation, resulting in a multitude of symptoms. This metric determines the total amount of iron in the blood. High amounts are indicative of iron overload, while low levels can indicate deficiency.

Stress hormones, such as cortisol, adrenaline and noradrenaline break down our tissue protein, which gets metabolized by the liver and excretes urea-nitrogen as a byproduct. Thus, a high BUN can indicate elevations in these mediators. A high BUN can also indicate impaired kidney function as they are responsible for excreting the excess nitrogen from the blood.

Absolute Eosinophils measures the number of eosinophils. Eosinophils are immune cells that respond to infections caused by parasites and play a role in allergic reactions. Elevated levels can indicate parasitic infection or allergy, while low levels are indicative of chronic stress.

The Vibrant Wellness Micronutrient Profile is a functional blood test that measures both extracellular (serum) and intracellular (lymphocyte) nutrient status, giving a deeper view than standard serum labs alone. This dual approach helps identify not just current nutrient levels, but also how well nutrients are getting into cells and being utilized for metabolism. It covers vitamins, minerals, amino acids, antioxidants, fatty acids, and metabolites, providing a broad picture of nutritional sufficiency and functional reserves. It’s useful for tailoring diet and supplementation strategies, uncovering hidden deficiencies linked to fatigue, poor immunity, or chronic disease, and monitoring progress over time.

Diagnostic: NutrEval ($429 base, more for add ons)
Implication: The Genova NutrEval is a comprehensive analysis done using both urine and in blood, yielding 125 biomarkers. It covers micronutrient status and 4 heavy metals, in addition to urinary organic acids and plasma amino acids. Organic acids are intermediates of metabolism, and their measurement in urine, alongside plasma amino acids, is used to evaluate specific errors in metabolism. These errors can be any malfunctioning in any metabolic process: from the generation of energy from carbohydrates or fats to the synthesis of certain neurotransmitters and more. Errors of metabolism can result in any symptom and may not be identified by standard bloodwork.

Rheumatoid factor (RF) is an autoantibody initially identified in rheumatoid arthritis. It targets the Fc region of IgG, with various RFs recognizing different sections of this region. RF and IgG combine to form immune complexes, which play a role in the disease by promoting chronic inflammation and causing joint damage in the synovium and cartilage.

The DUTCH test yields an in-depth analysis on steroid hormone status. The test utilizes urine samples and probes for dozens of steroid hormones and their metabolites, providing a full picture of an individual’s hormones tied to sexual function, stress, cognitive function and more.

Mycotoxins are metabolites produced by fungi like mold, that can infest your home, vehicle or workspace as one of the most common environmental pollutants. These mycotoxins have wide ranging negative health effects by contributing to systemic inflammation, immune system dysregulation, bacterial overgrowth, gut hypersensitivity and mitochondrial dysfunction. The Mold IgE Panel analyzes the presence of immune reactivity to mycotoxins in blood.

Mycotoxins are metabolites produced by fungi like mold, that can infest your home, vehicle or workspace as one of the most common environmental pollutants. These mycotoxins have wide ranging negative health effects by contributing to systemic inflammation, immune system dysregulation, bacterial overgrowth, gut hypersensitivity and mitochondrial dysfunction. The Mold Toxicity Test analyzes the presence of mycotoxins in urine.

Accumulation of heavy metals can result in a variety of seemingly unexplainable symptoms. Precision Point Diagnostics’ Heavy Metal Urinalysis can be collected over 24 hours, or over a specified collection time, to detect toxic metal exposures. Urinary heavy metals tend to be higher when heavy metal exposure is longer term, and can be good to test in conjunction with blood heavy metals for a more comprehensive view.

Accumulation of heavy metals in the body can result in a variety of seemingly unexplainable symptoms. These metals can drive oxidative stress, inflammation and can disrupt normal cellular function by inhibiting enzymes and signaling pathways. This heavy metal panel determines the levels of the key heavy metals in circulation, indicative of a relatively recent exposure to heavy metals.

Immunoglobulin E (IgE) is a type of antibody that plays a critical role in mediating allergies and the response to infection. Elevated levels can suggest the presence of an underlying allergy or chronic bacterial, fungal or parasitic infection.

Lipoprotein(a) is a carrier of various fat soluble substances, primarily triglycerides and cholesterol. It is considered a risk factor for heart disease due to pro-inflammatory and pro-clotting properties. One of its primary functions is to sequester oxidized LDL, a toxic byproduct of excess polyunsaturated fat intake and oxidative damage. Elevated levels can thus be a proxy for these processes.

Homocysteine is a compound that is an intermediate of the methylation process. Methylation is critical to synthesizing and modifying DNA, neurotransmitters, and other important molecules within the body. Homocysteine can accumulate when there are deficiencies of vitamins B2, B6, B9 and B12, or nutrients choline, betaine, or glycine. It has numerous deleterious effects, contributing to oxidative stress, inflammation, mitochondrial dysfunction and excess excitation in the brain. Elevated homocysteine has been found in numerous inflammatory conditions.

The D-dimer test measures a protein fragment produced when a blood clot is broken down. Elevated levels suggest active clot formation and degradation, making it useful for detecting conditions like deep vein thrombosis (DVT), pulmonary embolism (PE), or ongoing microclotting, as seen in long COVID. While a high D-dimer indicates clot activity, it’s nonspecific and should be interpreted alongside other clinical findings.

The erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR or sed rate) measures how quickly red blood cells, in anticoagulated whole blood, settle at the bottom of a standardized tube over the course of one hour. It serves as a non-specific indicator of inflammation. ESR is affected by the clumping of red blood cells, known as aggregation. Blood plasma proteins, primarily fibrinogen, encourage the formation of red cell clusters. While aggregation occurs even at normal physiological fibrinogen levels, these levels tend to rise during inflammatory processes, resulting in an elevated ESR.

C-reactive protein, a substance produced by the liver in response to inflammation in the body. It is a non-specific marker of inflammation and is used to evaluate the presence and intensity of inflammation. The CRP test helps assess the severity of an inflammation that may drive symptoms.
Suspected bacterial, fungal or parasitic overgrowths, which contribute to numerous systemic and gastrointestinal symptoms, can be detected using stool samples.
This is an enzyme that plays a critical role in maintaining intestinal homeostasis, reducing inflammation, and detoxifying endotoxin. Elevated levels suggest liver damage or intestinal inflammation.

Absolute Monocytes measures the number of monocytes. Monocytes are immune cells that can mature into phagocytes, which are cells that engulf and destroy pathogens. Elevated levels can be suggestive of an underlying infection.

Monocytes measures the percentage of white blood cells that are monocytes. Monocytes are immune cells that can mature into phagocytes, which are cells that engulf and destroy pathogens. Elevated levels can be suggestive of an underlying infection.
Absolute myelocytes measures the number of myelocytes. Myelocytes are a type of young and immature forms of white blood cells that are yet to differentiate into their final cell type.

Myelocytes measures the percentage of white blood cells that are myelocytes. Myelocytes are a type of young and immature forms of white blood cells that are yet to differentiate into their final cell type.
Absolute metamyelocytes measures the number of myelocytes. Metamyelocytes are a type of young and immature forms of white blood cells that are yet to differentiate into their final cell type.

Metamyelocytes measures the percentage of white blood cells that are metamyelocytes. Metamyelocytes are a type of young and immature forms of white blood cells that are yet to differentiate into their final cell type.
Absolute promyelocytes measures the number of myelocytes. Promyelocytes are a type of young and immature forms of white blood cells that are yet to differentiate into their final cell type.

Promyelocytes measures the percentage of white blood cells that are metamyelocytes. Metamyelocytes are a type of young and immature forms of white blood cells that are yet to differentiate into their final cell type.

Absolute nucleated red blood cells measures the number of nucleated red blood cells. Nucleated red blood cells are red blood cell precursors. A high level of nucleated red blood cells suggests a high demand for the genesis of new red blood cells, often indicative of conditions including anemia, myelofibrosis, thalassemia, miliary tuberculosis, cancers involving bone marrow (myelomas, leukemias, lymphomas), and in chronic hypoxemia.

Nucleated red blood cells measures the percentage of red blood cells that are nucleated. Nucleated red blood cells are red blood cell precursors. A high level of nucleated red blood cells suggests a high demand for the genesis of new red blood cells, often indicative of conditions including anemia, myelofibrosis, thalassemia, miliary tuberculosis, cancers involving bone marrow (myelomas, leukemias, lymphomas), and in chronic hypoxemia.

LDL cholesterol is the most common circulating cholesterol containing lipoprotein, which can be thought of as a "boat" that carries around fat soluble substances in the blood. HDL is another type of lipoprotein that primarily carries cholesterol. If these lipoproteins become too rich in triglycerides, which are the types of fat stored in fat tissue, they become smaller and more dense. A predominance of small, dense LDL particles (subclass pattern B) is linked to higher levels of triglyceride-rich lipoproteins and lower HDL levels compared to individuals with predominantly larger LDL particles (pattern A). These smaller lipoproteins are more susceptible to the damage that causes atherosclerosis or the clotting of arteries.

Ochratoxin A is a type of mycotoxin, a compound produced my mold and other fungi. Exposure can be from airborne sources such as mold accumulation in water damaged buildings, or from food. It can induce mitochondrial dysfunction, oxidative stress and increase endotoxin levels systemically. It is capable of causing liver damage and increasing inflammation in the gut.
